summary: Gruber revisits his criticism of Apple's inconsistent use of brushed metal windows in Mac OS X, honestly admitting he's unsure whether the inconsistency truly matters or if it's merely an aesthetic preference. He engages with Mike Manzano's counterargument that the differences are purely cosmetic and don't affect widget behavior or placement. While conceding the point has merit, Gruber then pivots to an actual usability complaint: brushed metal windows look nearly identical whether active or in the background, making it easy to mistake which window has focus and invoke wrong keyboard shortcuts.
key_points: Apple has no consistent policy governing brushed metal window usage, and third-party developers have followed Apple's inconsistent example rather than the HIG
The strongest counterargument is that consistency only matters when it affects usability, and brushed metal differences are merely cosmetic
Brushed metal windows fail to visually distinguish between active and background states, unlike standard Aqua windows which use gradient, darkness, and depth cues
The argument that brushed metal helps identify apps in Exposé doesn't scale — taken to its logical conclusion, every app would need its own custom window style
The active/background window distinction is fundamental to the Mac experience and should be visually significant, not subtle
money_quotes: This isn't a retraction, per se, but an admission of vacillation. I just don't know.

Maybe it is just aesthetics — merely the color of pixels, rather than how the interface truly works — and I'm looking for serious implications that aren't there, simply because I happen to think brushed metal windows look stupid.
The texture of brushed metal windows is exactly the same when they're in the foreground and background.
The logical conclusion of this line of thinking is that every app should use its own custom window style.
The active window is an essential concept in the Macintosh user experience.
If there is a case to be made for brushed metal, usability certainly isn't it.
I've received more email this week from brushed metal aficionados than I have from gloating Red Sox fans.
key_insight: Even when the broader consistency debate is uncertain, brushed metal has a concrete usability flaw: it fails to visually distinguish active windows from background ones, undermining a fundamental element of the Mac interface.
